 ❯ We need to begin by trimming the claude.md file carefully and remember that anything we trim out needs to be moved somewhere were you can easily recall it quickly and often.

 Read CLAUDE.md (top through Current State) and SCOUT_PLAN.md (top through "YOU ARE HERE") and continue Session 62 from the exact next step listed.
 ▎
 ▎ The first action is: cd /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout && .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute
 ▎

 ▎ This runs Tuesday's 17-contact batch of the diocesan drip (~2.7 hours wall clock, 5-15 min jittered sleeps between POSTs, live Outreach writes). If you want Monday's 17 caught up first, run --force-day

 ▎ 2026-04-13 before the Tuesday run.

 ▎

 ▎ Before each POST the script verifies the target sequence is still enabled, dedups via find_prospect_by_email, pre-checks existing sequenceState membership, creates the prospect if new, and adds it to the

 ▎ right diocesan sequence. State file at data/diocesan_drip_state.json drives which day runs; the script is resumable on crash.

 ▎

 ▎ After the batch completes, run .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --verify to confirm live Outreach counts match the state file.

 ▎

 ▎ Per Rule 19: never surface Outreach backend numeric IDs in chat. Translate prospect IDs to "First Last (email@domain)", sequence IDs to the diocesan name, mailbox IDs to "your mailbox", and omit

 ▎ sequenceState IDs entirely.

 ▎

 ▎ Per Rule 18: before writing any new prospect-add code, grep tools/outreach_client.py + check docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + grep git log --since=120days. The canonical library functions already exist.

 [<u[>1u[>4;2m

 Trim plan for CLAUDE.md (46K → ~20K target)

 What to move:

 - Session 61 narrative (lines ~8–150 of CLAUDE.md, ~18K chars) → append as ## Session 61 section in SCOUT_HISTORY.md . Already duplicated in SCOUT_PLAN.md lines 1–91, so this is pure deduplication.

 - Session 60 narrative (lines ~152–230, ~8K chars) → append as ## Session 60 in SCOUT_HISTORY.md . Also duplicated in SCOUT_PLAN.md .

 - Session 61 narrative (lines ~8–150 of CLAUDE.md, ~18K chars) → append as ## Session 61 section in SCOUT_HISTORY.md . Already duplicated in SCOUT_PLAN.md lines 1–91, so this is pure deduplication.
 0 152–230, ~8K chars) → append as ## Session 60 in SCOUT_HISTORY.md . Also duplicated in SCOUT_PLAN.md .

 What replaces it: a ~25-line CURRENT STATE block with (a) repo state in 2 sentences, (b) exact next action, (c) pointers: "Session 61 full narrative → SCOUT_HISTORY.md §Session 61", "active plan →
 SCOUT_PLAN.md §YOU ARE HERE".

 What stays verbatim in CLAUDE.md: LOAD-BEARING REFERENCES, all 4 PREFLIGHT CHECKLISTS, CRITICAL RULES 1–19, WHAT SCOUT IS, REFERENCE MATERIAL pointers. Nothing load-bearing gets lost — it all already lives in
 docs/SCOUT_RULES.md , docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md , memory/*.md , and (after the move) SCOUT_HISTORY.md . The SessionStart hook already auto-injects SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md so the amnesia fix is not affected.

 Recall paths after trim:

 - Next-session context: CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE pointer → SCOUT_HISTORY.md §Session 61

 - Active plan detail: SCOUT_PLAN.md §YOU ARE HERE (unchanged)

 - Rules/preflights: still in CLAUDE.md (auto-loaded)

 - Capabilities inventory: SessionStart hook (auto-loaded)

 - Next-session context: CLAUDE.md CURRENT STATE pointer → SCOUT_HISTORY.md §Session 61
 Active plan detail: SCOUT_PLAN.md §YOU ARE HERE (unchanged)
 Rules/prefligh s: still in CLAUDE.md (auto-loaded)
 Capabilities inventory: SessionStart hook (auto-loaded)

 Drip question

 Today is Tue Apr 14. Monday's 17-contact batch was skipped (you EOD'd Apr 13 night). Options:

 1. Tue only — run --execute once, 17 contacts, ~2.7hr. Monday stays deferred.

 2. Mon catchup then Tue — run --force-day 2026-04-13 , then --execute . ~34 contacts, ~5.5hr back-to-back.

 Which do you want? And OK to kick the drip off in the background so I can trim CLAUDE.md in parallel while it runs?

 1 # SCOUT — Claude Code Reference
 2 - *Last updated: 2026-04-14 — End of Session 61 (two bodies of work: Research Engine Round 1 shipped but Level 3 Waverly gate failed and flags are parked default-OFF; diocesan drip library + amnes
 - ia fix shipped including SessionStart hook that auto-loads docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md; 63 diocesan contacts assigned across Mon-Thu, canary verified clean, Batch 1-4 live writes start Tue Apr 14
 - morning via `scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute`. 9 commits on main.)*
 2 + *Last updated: 2026-04-14 — Session 62 opened. S61 shipped 9 commits (research engine R1 parked default-OFF, diocesan drip library live, amnesia root-cause fix + SessionStart hook). Diocesan dri
 + p mid-execution.*
 4 ---
 6 ## CURRENT STATE — update this after each session

 8 - **Session 61 had two distinct bodies of work.** First half: Research Engine Round 1 code shipped across 4 commits but Level 3 Waverly A/B failed the verified quality gate — flags are parked defa

 - ult-OFF, Round 1.1 planning needed. Second half: diocesan drip library shipped (promoted S38/S43 ephemeral prospect-add patterns into canonical tools/outreach_client.py + tools/prospect_loader.p

 - y), 63 diocesan contacts assigned across Mon-Thu buckets, throwaway canary verified clean in the Chicago diocesan sequence. Amnesia root-cause fix also shipped: new docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md in

 - ventory auto-injected into every session via a SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json. **Next session's first action:** `.venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute` to run Tuesday

 - 's live batch (17 contacts, ~2.7 hours).

 8 + **Session-narrative history has been moved to `SCOUT_HISTORY.md`. Active plan detail lives in `SCOUT_PLAN.md §YOU ARE HERE`. This section stays ≤30 lines.**

 10 + **Where we are right now (Session 62 opening):**

 11 + - Diocesan drip is actively loading 63 contacts across Mon–Thu into sequences 2008–2013. Canonical path: `scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute`. State file: `data/diocesan_drip_state.json` (63 con

 + tacts, assigned round-robin, VERIFIED-first within each diocese). Canary passed S61; live writes begin S62.

 12 + - Research Engine Round 1 flags are shipped but default OFF (Level 3 Waverly A/B failed `verified_quality_gate`). Production `agent/main.py` is byte-for-byte v1. Round 1.1 planning is carryover

 + work; do NOT enable flags without a fresh plan.

 13 + - Repo is 1 commit ahead of `origin/main` (transcript auto-save `029fdc8`). Working tree clean except `.DS_Store`.

 14 +

 15 + **Exact next action (Session 62 start):**

 16 + ```bash

 17 + cd /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 18 + .venv/bin/python scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute # today's bucket

 19 + # optional: --force-day 2026-04-13 to catch up Monday first

 20 + ```

 21 + Script is idempotent on crash, dedups via `find_prospect_by_email`, 5–15 min jittered sleeps between POSTs, ~2.7 h wall clock per 17-contact day. After Thursday's batch → `--verify`.

 22 +

 23 + **For full narrative of what S60/S61 shipped, failed, and why:** `SCOUT_HISTORY.md §Session 60` and `§Session 61`.

 24 + **For the active, detailed plan (commits, tables, Round 1.1 options):** `SCOUT_PLAN.md §YOU ARE HERE`.

 25 +

 26 + **Session 62 carryover (non-drip, load by demand):**

 27 + 1. Research Engine Round 1.1 plan (per-URL content MERGE, not dedup). Plan-mode session required.

 28 + 2. BUG 5 code fix in `tools/research_engine.py::_target_match_params` (shared-city gap). Plan-mode session.

 29 + 3. 9 pending dioceses review (Pittsburgh/OKC/Omaha) — blocked on BUG 5.

 30 + 4. Optional: F9 compliance scanner query redesign, LA archdiocese restart, IN/OK/TN CSTA hand-curation.

 31 + 5. Deferred: 1,245 cold_license_request + 247 winback March backlogs.

 32 + 6. Future: wire `prospect_loader.execute_load_plan` into `_on_prospect_research_complete`.

 1 shell

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the
 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot
 blasting 17 creates i 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self
 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your
 own memory).

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Now committing the trim:

 1 # SCOUT — Claude Code Reference

 2 - *Last updated: 2026-04-14 — End of Session 61 (two bodies of work: Research Engine Round 1 shipped but Level 3 Waverly gate failed and flags are parked default-OFF; diocesan drip library + amnes

 - ia fix shipped including SessionStart hook that auto-loads docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md; 63 diocesan contacts assigned across Mon-Thu, canary verified clean, Batch 1-4 live writes start Tue Apr 14

 - morning via `scripts/diocesan_drip.py --execute`. 9 commits on main.)*

 2 + *Last updated: 2026-04-14 — Session 62 opened. S61 shipped 9 commits (research engine R1 parked default-OFF, diocesan drip library live, amnesia root-cause fix + SessionStart hook). Diocesan dri

 + p mid-execution.*

 4 ---

 6 ## CURRENT STATE — update this after each session

 8 - **Session 61 had two distinct bodies of work.** First half: Research Engine Round 1 code shipped across 4 commits but Level 3 Waverly A/B failed the verified quality gate — flags are parked defa

 - ult-OFF, Round 1.1 planning needed. Second half: diocesan drip library shipped (promoted S38/S43 ephemeral prospect-add patterns into canonical tools/outreach_client.py + tools/prospect_loader.p

 - y), 63 diocesan contacts assigned across Mon-Thu buckets, throwaway canary verified clean in the Chicago diocesan sequence. Amnesia root-cause fix also shipped: new docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md in

 Drip status: Monday batch running in background. I'll get notified when it finishes (~2.7h), then auto-start Tuesday. I'll also update you if anything looks off in the log.

 Want me to commit the trim as a standalone commit, or hold?

 ❯ what is taking 2.7hours?

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 at the beginning of every session to make sure no amnesia, drift, or any other wierd errors happen.

 What are you thoughts on that idea? is it a good idea or do you already do that anyway?

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Now committing the trim:

 Drip status: Monday batch running in background. I'll get notified when it finishes (~2.7h), then auto-start Tuesday. I'll also update you if anything looks off in the log.

 Want me to commit the trim as a standalone commit, or hold?

 ❯ what is taking 2.7hours?

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 at the beginning of every session to make sure no amnesia, drift, or any other wierd errors happen.

 What are you thoughts on that idea? is it a good idea or do you already do that anyway?

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Now committing the trim:

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't
 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that
 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.
 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.
 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Drip status: Monday batch running in background. I'll get notified when it finishes (~2.7h), then auto-start Tuesday. I'll also update you if anything looks off in the log.

 Want me to commit the trim as a standalone commit, or hold?

 ❯ what is taking 2.7hours?

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 at the beginning of every session to make sure no amnesia, drift, or any other wierd errors happen.

 What are you thoughts on that idea? is it a good idea or do you already do that anyway?

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Now committing the trim:

 ⏺ Monday restarting. 15 pending × ~20s avg sleep ≈ 5 min. I'll get notified when it finishes, then auto-run Tuesday. Now to your real question — the retention problem.

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 ❯ yes

 1 #!/bin/bash

 2 # Scout session context loader — runs on every SessionStart for Scout repo.

 3 # Cats everything load-bearing into session context so Claude has full proje

 4 # memory on day 1 of any session, not just the compact CLAUDE.md pointer.

 5 #

 6 # Relies on prompt caching — first read of the day pays full cost, every

 7 # subsequent session turn hits the cache. Total ~212K tokens (~21% of 1M win

 dow).

 8 #

 9 # Only fires when cwd is inside the Scout repo.

 11 set -u

 12 REPO=/Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 14 # Fast no-op if we're not in Scout (matcher is regex-based but be defensive)

 15 case " $PWD " in

 16 " $REPO " *) ;;

 17 *) exit 0 ;;

 18 esac

 20 emit_file () {

 21 local label= " $1 "

 22 local path= " $2 "

 23 if [ -f " $path " ]; then

 24 echo ""

 25 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 26 cat " $path "

 28 }

 30 emit_glob () {

 31 local label= " $1 "

 32 local pattern= " $2 "

 33 local any=0

 34 for f in $pattern ; do

 35 [ -f " $f " ] || continue

 36 if [ " $any " -eq 0 ]; then

 37 echo ""

 38 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 39 any=1

 41 echo ""

 42 echo "--- $(basename " $f " ) ---"

 43 cat " $f "

 44 done

 45 }

 47 echo "# Scout full-context session load"

 48 echo "# Injected by ~/.claude/hooks/scout-session-load.sh on SessionStart."

 49 echo "# Goal: zero amnesia. Everything load-bearing is here, including full"

 50 echo "# per-session history and all user-level memory entries."

 52 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md"

 53 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_RULES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_RULES.md"

 54 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md"

 55 emit_file "SCOUT_HISTORY.md" " $REPO /SCOUT_HISTORY.md"

 57 # User-level memory files (behavioral feedback, project notes, references).

 58 # MEMORY.md itself is already loaded by Claude Code; this cats the file bodi

 59 # so the index entries become actually-readable content.

 60 MEMDIR= "/Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/

 memory"

 61 emit_glob "user-level memory/*.md" " $MEMDIR /*.md"

 63 exit 0

 Drip status: Monday batch running in background. I'll get notified when it finishes (~2.7h), then auto-start Tuesday. I'll also update you if anything looks off in the log.

 Want me to commit the trim as a standalone commit, or hold?

 ❯ what is taking 2.7hours?

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 at the beginning of every session to make sure no amnesia, drift, or any other wierd errors happen.

 What are you thoughts on that idea? is it a good idea or do you already do that anyway?

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 ⏺ Monday restarting. 15 pending × ~20s avg sleep ≈ 5 min. I'll get notified when it finishes, then auto-run Tuesday. Now to your real question — the retention problem.

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 ❯ yes

 1 #!/bin/bash

 2 # Scout session context loader — runs on every SessionStart for Scout repo.

 3 # Cats everything load-bearing into session context so Claude has full project

 4 # memory on day 1 of any session, not just the compact CLAUDE.md pointer.

 5 #

 6 # Relies on prompt caching — first read of the day pays full cost, every

 7 # subsequent session turn hits the cache. Total ~212K tokens (~21% of 1M window).

 8 #

 9 # Only fires when cwd is inside the Scout repo.

 11 set -u

 12 REPO=/Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 14 # Fast no-op if we're not in Scout (matcher is regex-based but be defensive)

 15 case " $PWD " in

 16 " $REPO " *) ;;

 17 *) exit 0 ;;

 18 esac

 20 emit_file () {

 21 local label= " $1 "

 22 local path= " $2 "

 23 if [ -f " $path " ]; then

 24 echo ""

 25 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 26 cat " $path "

 28 }

 30 emit_glob () {

 31 local label= " $1 "

 32 local pattern= " $2 "

 33 local any=0

 34 for f in $pattern ; do

 35 [ -f " $f " ] || continue

 36 if [ " $any " -eq 0 ]; then

 37 echo ""

 38 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 39 any=1

 41 echo ""

 42 echo "--- $(basename " $f " ) ---"

 43 cat " $f "

 44 done

 45 }

 47 echo "# Scout full-context session load"

 48 echo "# Injected by ~/.claude/hooks/scout-session-load.sh on SessionStart."

 49 echo "# Goal: zero amnesia. Everything load-bearing is here, including full"

 50 echo "# per-session history and all user-level memory entries."

 52 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md"

 53 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_RULES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_RULES.md"

 54 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md"

 55 emit_file "SCOUT_HISTORY.md" " $REPO /SCOUT_HISTORY.md"

 57 # User-level memory files (behavioral feedback, project notes, references).

 58 # MEMORY.md itself is already loaded by Claude Code; this cats the file bodies

 59 # so the index entries become actually-readable content.

 60 MEMDIR= "/Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory"

 61 emit_glob "user-level memory/*.md" " $MEMDIR /*.md"

 63 exit 0

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 1 #!/bin/bash

 2 # Scout session context loader — runs on every SessionStart for Scout repo.

 3 # Cats everything load-bearing into session context so Claude has full project

 4 # memory on day 1 of any session, not just the compact CLAUDE.md pointer.

 5 #

 6 # Relies on prompt caching — first read of the day pays full cost, every

 7 # subsequent session turn hits the cache. Total ~212K tokens (~21% of 1M window).

 8 #

 9 # Only fires when cwd is inside the Scout repo.

 11 set -u

 12 REPO=/Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout

 14 # Fast no-op if we're not in Scout (matcher is regex-based but be defensive)

 15 case " $PWD " in

 16 " $REPO " *) ;;

 17 *) exit 0 ;;

 18 esac

 20 emit_file () {

 21 local label= " $1 "

 22 local path= " $2 "

 23 if [ -f " $path " ]; then

 24 echo ""

 25 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 26 cat " $path "

 28 }

 30 emit_glob () {

 31 local label= " $1 "

 32 local pattern= " $2 "

 33 local any=0

 34 for f in $pattern ; do

 35 [ -f " $f " ] || continue

 36 if [ " $any " -eq 0 ]; then

 37 echo ""

 38 echo "===== ${label} ====="

 39 any=1

 41 echo ""

 42 echo "--- $(basename " $f " ) ---"

 43 cat " $f "

 44 done

 45 }

 47 echo "# Scout full-context session load"

 48 echo "# Injected by ~/.claude/hooks/scout-session-load.sh on SessionStart."

 49 echo "# Goal: zero amnesia. Everything load-bearing is here, including full"

 50 echo "# per-session history and all user-level memory entries."

 52 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md"

 53 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_RULES.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_RULES.md"

 54 emit_file "docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md" " $REPO /docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md"

 55 emit_file "SCOUT_HISTORY.md" " $REPO /SCOUT_HISTORY.md"

 57 # User-level memory files (behavioral feedback, project notes, references).

 58 # MEMORY.md itself is already loaded by Claude Code; this cats the file bodies

 59 # so the index entries become actually-readable content.

 60 MEMDIR= "/Users/stevenadkins/.claude/projects/-Users-stevenadkins-Code-Scout/memory"

 61 emit_glob "user-level memory/*.md" " $MEMDIR /*.md"

 63 exit 0

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 ❯ what is taking 2.7hours?

 Yes commit the trim but I want to make sure you have a rule where you are to read wherever we have put all the trimmed stuff out of the claude.md over all these sessions. I want you to make sure to read that

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 ⚠ Large CLAUDE.md will impact performance (46.0k chars > 40.0k) · /memory to edit

 ⏺ What's taking 2.7h

 Not the POSTs — each one is ~2 seconds (refresh token → check sequence enabled → find prospect by email → create prospect → add to sequence). The 2.7h comes from deliberate 5–15 min jittered sleeps between the

 17 POSTs . Mean ~10 min × 16 gaps ≈ 160 min, plus ~2 min of actual HTTP. The sleeps are there to (a) not burst Outreach's rate limits, (b) look like a human loading prospects through the day rather than a bot

 blasting 17 creates in 30 seconds, (c) give you a chance to abort between POSTs if something looks wrong in the first few. It's intentional pacing, not slow code.

 Your "always read the trimmed content at session start" idea

 Honest answer: the spirit is right but the literal version would re-bloat your context window. Here's the reality check:

 - docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (362 lines) — already auto-loaded every session via the SessionStart hook you set up in S61. ✅

 - docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (301 lines) — NOT auto-loaded today. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (212 lines) — NOT auto-loaded. Load-bearing. Cheap to add.

 - SCOUT_HISTORY.md (1,686 lines and growing) — NOT auto-loaded, and should not be fully auto-loaded . It's bigger than the thing we just trimmed out of CLAUDE.md. Auto-loading it undoes the trim.

 - memory/*.md — already auto-loaded at session start (that's the MEMORY.md index mechanism).

 And to your other question: no, I don't already do this. Without a hook forcing it, there's no mechanism that makes me read a file before I start working. "Remember to read X every session" as a note-to-self

 is exactly the kind of process rule that fails — it's how we got the S38/S43 amnesia in the first place. Code enforcement beats process rules (that's feedback_code_enforcement_beats_process_rules.md from your

 own memory).

 My recommendation (structural fix, not a process rule):

 Extend the existing SessionStart hook in ~/.claude/settings.json so it cats three files instead of one:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md (current)

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md (add)

 3. The last 2 sessions of SCOUT_HISTORY.md only — via something like awk '/^## Session 6[01] /,0' SCOUT_HISTORY.md — so you get the most-recent narrative context without paying for all 56 sessions.

 That gives you ~900 lines of auto-loaded stable context per session instead of the current ~360, without ballooning. SCOUT_REFERENCE.md (repo tree, env vars, tool registry) is more of a

 reference-when-you-need-it — I'd leave it on-demand via grep unless you disagree.

 Same mechanism, three files instead of one, scoped window on the history. Good idea, I just want to scope it so it doesn't defeat the trim.

 Want me to wire this up after I commit the trim? Or should the hook change be its own session's work?

 Now committing the trim:

 ⏺ Monday restarting. 15 pending × ~20s avg sleep ≈ 5 min. I'll get notified when it finishes, then auto-run Tuesday. Now to your real question — the retention problem.

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 1 #!/bin/bash

 2 # Scout session context loader — runs on every SessionStart for Scout repo.

 3 # Cats everything load-bearing into session context so Claude has full project

 4 # memory on day 1 of any session, not just the compact CLAUDE.md pointer.

 5 #

 6 # Relies on prompt caching — first read of the day pays full cost, every

 7 # subsequent session turn hits the cache. Total ~212K tokens (~21% of 1M window).

 8 #

 9 # Only fires when cwd is inside the Scout repo.

 ⏺ Monday restarting. 15 pending × ~20s avg sleep ≈ 5 min. I'll get notified when it finishes, then auto-run Tuesday. Now to your real question — the retention problem.

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.

 ⎿   Wrote 63 lines to ../../.claude/hooks/scout-session-load.sh

 1 #!/bin/bash

 2 # Scout session context loader — runs on every SessionStart for Scout repo.

 3 # Cats everything load-bearing into session context so Claude has full project

 4 # memory on day 1 of any session, not just the compact CLAUDE.md pointer.

 5 #

 6 # Relies on prompt caching — first read of the day pays full cost, every

 7 # subsequent session turn hits the cache. Total ~212K tokens (~21% of 1M window).

 8 #

 9 # Only fires when cwd is inside the Scout repo.

 Status

 Drip: Monday batch running cleanly (~9/15 remaining contacts done, should finish in ~3 min). Tuesday queued to auto-run after Monday. New jitter committed as 3b97046 .

 CLAUDE.md trim: committed 6da2a8f , pushed. 46K → 20K bytes.

 Full-context SessionStart hook: wired at ~/.claude/hooks/scout-session-load.sh , pointed to from ~/.claude/settings.json . Cats:

 1. docs/SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md

 2. docs/SCOUT_RULES.md

 3. docs/SCOUT_REFERENCE.md

 4. SCOUT_HISTORY.md (full, all 56+ sessions)

 5. All 107 user-level memory files in ~/.claude/projects/.../memory/*.md

 Total injected per session: ~610KB / ~150K tokens / 15% of your 1M window. First read pays cold-cache cost, every turn after hits the cache at ~10% price. The hook is cwd -gated — it only fires when you're

 inside /Users/stevenadkins/Code/Scout , so other projects don't get spammed.

 What this gives you: starting with Session 63 (your next /exit → new session), I will open every conversation with the full Scout history, every rule, every memory, every capability already in my head. No

 amnesia surface for anything that's already been written down. Session 62 (this one) won't see the new hook since it's already running — the next session will.

 What this does NOT solve:

 - Amnesia for things that were never written down (still requires discipline)

 - Drift during long sessions as older context gets pushed out (context is finite even at 1M)

 - The specific S38/S43 "ephemeral script" failure mode (Rule 18 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES cover that one)

 Nothing to commit for the hook — it lives in ~/.claude/ , not the repo. Want me to add a pointer in CLAUDE.md documenting that the hook exists, so if you ever reset your machine or move to a new one you know

 what to rebuild?

 ⏺ Monday restarting. 15 pending × ~20s avg sleep ≈ 5 min. I'll get notified when it finishes, then auto-run Tuesday. Now to your real question — the retention problem.

 Creative approaches to "remember everything like I do"

 I did think about this harder. Here are the real options, ranked by how well they match your goal:

 Option 1 — Just load everything. You're on Opus 4.6 with a 1M context window.

 Full scope if I sum it: SCOUT_HISTORY.md 1,686 lines + SCOUT_RULES.md 301 + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md 212 + SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md 362 + all memory/*.md (~30 files × ~100 lines) ≈ ~6,000 lines total ≈ ~90K tokens .

 That's 9% of the 1M window . Completely fine.

 the cost. Same story for every subsequent session within a 5-minute window.

 This is the honest answer to your question. Your context window is big enough that the "brutal trim and grep on demand" strategy we've been using for ~20 sessions was optimized for a constraint that doesn't

 exist anymore. You have 1M tokens and you're asking me to remember everything — the simplest solution is to literally load everything.

 Option 2 — Hierarchical auto-load (what I proposed before). Load SCOUT_CAPABILITIES + SCOUT_RULES + last 2 sessions of history. ~900 lines. Cheaper but lossier. Doesn't match your "remember like I do" ask.

 Option 3 — A regenerated "Scout Overview" file I maintain at every EOD. I'd produce a ~500-line dense synthesis of the entire project state (architecture, active projects, what's live, known bugs, lessons that

 shape current judgment calls, active people/stakeholders, budget state). Stable shape, not a historical log. It's the "Scout mental model on one page" I'd build for an incoming engineer. Auto-loaded via hook.

 This is what you'd want if you were on a small model — but you're not.

 Option 4 — Keyword-triggered injection via User Prompt Submit hooks. When you mention "research engine" in your prompt, a hook fires that cats research_engine memory files into context. Already works for Vercel

 (you saw the auto-injection at session start with "workflow" matched). But requires careful tuning and is easy to miss.

 Option 5 — MCP-backed knowledge retrieval. Build a tiny MCP server exposing scout_recall(topic) that does semantic search over the entire project's docs + history + memory. Claude calls it like any other tool

 when it needs context. Most powerful, most complex. Probably overkill.

 My recommendation: Option 1 + Option 3 together, in that order of effort.

 Near-term (today, 5 min of work): extend the SessionStart hook to cat everything — SCOUT_CAPABILITIES.md + SCOUT_RULES.md + SCOUT_REFERENCE.md + SCOUT_HISTORY.md + all memory/*.md . ~90K tokens, prompt-cached

 after first read, 9% of your 1M window. Closest thing to "remember everything like you do" that exists without writing new code.

 Longer-term: also introduce Option 3 — an EOD ritual where I regenerate a docs/SCOUT_OVERVIEW.md dense synthesis. The SessionStart hook loads that one file as a "here's where we stand" preamble before the raw

 docs kick in. Best of both worlds: the dense synthesis frames the raw context.

 One caveat worth saying out loud: auto-loading everything is not a substitute for the preflight checklists or the Rule 18 "grep before writing" discipline. Those exist because loading ≠ reading ≠ reasoning.

 Even with everything in context, I can still drift or miss. The hook makes amnesia structurally impossible ; the rules make drift recoverable . You want both.

 Want me to wire up Option 1 (extend the hook to cat everything) right now? Then we can consider the regenerated overview as a Session 62+ ritual.
